title: Trends in University Department Preferences in Turkey (2018–2025)
text: OLAN HER YERE KENDİ CÜMLELERİNLE İNGİLİZCE YAZ. YAZILARI YAZINCA  SATIRINI SİL. TURNITIN AI KONTROLÜ VAR, KOPYALA YAPISTIRMA! ============================================================ –>

title: Trends in University Department Preferences in Turkey
text:
2.1 Data Source
The data is obtained from the `thestats` package in R, which scrapes data from the YÖK Atlas portal maintained by the Turkish Higher Education Council (YÖK). YÖK Atlas provides detailed statistics on university programs, student preferences, and placement results across Turkey.

title: Trends in University Department Preferences in Turkey
text:
2.2 General Information About Data
The dataset contains 25,652 rows and 198 variables. For this project, 10 key variables were selected:

year: Year the data was announced (2018, 2019, 2020)
type: Type of university (State or Private)
university: Name of the university
department: Name of the department/program
score_type: Entrance exam score type (SAY, EA, SÖZ, DİL)
quota: General quota of the program
placed: Number of students placed
lowest_score: Lowest placement score
highest_score: Highest placement score
num_choices: Number of students who chose this program

title: Trends in University Department Preferences in Turkey
text:
2.3 Reason of Choice
This dataset was selected for the following reasons:

It is directly accessible via R without any manual downloading or web scraping
It covers all universities and departments in Turkey, making it representative at the national level
It enables trend analysis across years and comparison across university types and score categories
The topic has direct relevance to Turkey’s higher education policy and planning

title: Trends in University Department Preferences in Turkey (2018–2025)
text:
2.2 Preprocessing
Since the three sources use slightly different column names and encoding conventions, we merged them into a single table and standardized department names by matching Turkish and English variants. University type labels (`unitur`) were also unified, and a `bolum_grubu` column was added to group programs into broader sub-field categories for easier comparison.


Data loading and preprocessing code
# Read the combined dataset
df <- fread("yokatlas_FINAL_CLEAN.csv", encoding = "UTF-8")

# Convert to tibble for tidyverse compatibility
df <- as_tibble(df)

# Quick structure check
cat("Dataset dimensions:", nrow(df), "rows x", ncol(df), "columns\n")


Dataset dimensions: 75145 rows x 11 columns


Data loading and preprocessing code
cat("Years covered:", paste(sort(unique(df$year)), collapse = ", "), "\n")


Years covered: 2018, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024, 2025 


Data loading and preprocessing code
cat("Categories:", paste(sort(unique(df$kategori)), collapse = ", "), "\n")


Categories: Diger, Dis_Hekimligi_Eczacilik, Hukuk, Mimarlik, Muhendislik, Ogretmenlik, Psikoloji, Tip 


Data loading and preprocessing code
# Save as .RData for reproducibility
save(df, file = "data.RData")


The processed dataset is stored in .RData format and can be accessed here.

title: Trends in University Department Preferences in Turkey (2018–2025)
text:
3.1 Exploratory Data Analysis


Score distribution by category
df |>
  filter(kategori != "Diger", year == 2024) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = kategori, y = puan, fill = kategori)) +
  geom_boxplot(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Score Distribution by Category (2024)",
       x = NULL, y = "Minimum Admission Score") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = 1))









The 2024 score distributions show considerable variation both across and within categories. Medicine and dentistry/pharmacy have the highest and most compact distributions, meaning most programs in these fields require similarly high scores. Law and engineering show wider spreads, with engineering in particular covering a very broad range — from around 0 to well above 400 — which points to the coexistence of very selective programs and much lower-threshold ones within the same category. Psychology's interquartile range sits mostly between 250 and 375, with a few outliers near zero that are likely low-quota private programs.


Missing data summary
df |>
  summarise(
    kontenjan_na = sum(is.na(kontenjan)),
    yerlesen_na = sum(is.na(yerlesen)),
    puan_na = sum(is.na(puan)),
    sira_na = sum(is.na(sira))
  ) |>
  print()


# A tibble: 1 × 4
  kontenjan_na yerlesen_na puan_na sira_na
         <int>       <int>   <int>   <int>
1        28148        4452       0    5357

title: Assignment 1
text:
Veri Bilimi ve Endüstri Mühendisliği Üzerine Sohbetler - Mustafa Baydoğan & Erdi Daşdemir
Bu videoda, Mustafa Baydoğan Hoca, kendi şirketi Algopoly’de karşılaştıkları gerçek problemlerden örnekler vererek sektör bazında bir mühendisin çözmesi gereken problemleri ve bu problemlerde veri madenciliği ve makina öğrenmesi gibi kavramların ne ölçüde karşımıza çıktığını bizlere aktarmaya çalıştı. Video boyunca bazıları kendi karşılaştığı problemler olmak üzere kereste, enerji, perakende gibi sektörlerden gerçek hayat problemlerini ve çözüm yollarını bize anlatarak anlatımını pekiştirdi ve bizlere gerçek hayat problemleri üzerinden sektörler arası bir bakış açısı sundu.

title: Assignment 1
text:
Veri Bilimi ve Endüstri Mühendisliği Üzerine Sohbetler - Mustafa Baydoğan & Erdi Daşdemir
Bu videoda, Mustafa Baydoğan Hoca, kendi şirketi Algopoly’de karşılaştıkları gerçek problemlerden örnekler vererek sektör bazında bir mühendisin çözmesi gereken problemleri ve bu problemlerde veri madenciliği ve makina öğrenmesi gibi kavramların ne ölçüde karşımıza çıktığını bizlere aktarmaya çalıştı. Video boyunca bazıları kendi karşılaştığı problemler olmak üzere kereste, enerji, perakende gibi sektörlerden gerçek hayat problemlerini ve çözüm yollarını bize anlatarak anlatımını pekiştirdi ve bizlere gerçek hayat problemleri üzerinden sektörler arası bir bakış açısı sundu.

title: Assignment 1
text:
b.)
Açık Uçlu Soru: Mustafa Baydoğan Hoca, ağaç işleri sektöründe çalışırken karşılaştığı problem üzerine bir endüstri mühendisinin bakış açısının bilgisayar mühendisliği ve diğer alanlardan nasıl ayrıştığını düşünmüştür ?
Cevap: Problemin çözümünde ilgili bilgisayar mühendislerinin problemi sadece data olarak yorumlamasına karşın Mustafa Hoca problemi daha derinlikli bir şekilde araştırıp, hatta o alandan mezun olan kişilerle konuşup sebebini irdelemiştir.
Çoktan Seçmeli Soru: Aşağıdakilerden hangisi geleneksel öğrenme
yöntemlerinin bir avantajıdır?
- A) Daha fazla parametre kullanması - B) Yapısal olmayan veriyle daha iyi çalışması - C) Yorumlanabilirlik ve gürbüzlük sağlaması - D) Öznitelik çıkarımını otomatik yapması
Cevap: C) Yorumlanabilirlik ve gürbüzlük sağlaması

title: Assignment 1

c.)

# compute_stats fonksiyonunu tanımladım
compute_stats <- function(x) {
  list(
    mean     = mean(x),
    median   = median(x),
    variance = var(x),
    IQR      = IQR(x),
    min      = min(x),
    max      = max(x)
  )
}

# for döngüsü ile her sütuna uyguladım
for (col_name in names(mtcars)) {
  
  cat("column:", col_name, "\n")
  
  print(compute_stats(mtcars[[col_name]]))
  
}

title: Trends in University Department Preferences in Turkey (2018–2025)
text:
2.1 Data Sources
Three data sources were combined to cover the full 2018–2025 period. The first is the `thestats` R package developed by Mustafa Cavus and Olgun Aydin, which provides structured YÖK Atlas data for the years 2018–2020. While this package makes early-period data conveniently accessible, its coverage ends at 2020. To fill the 2021 gap, we used a third-party dataset shared on GitHub by MorphaxTheDeveloper, which was compiled from YÖK Atlas records for that year. For the more recent years between 2022 and 2025, we pulled data directly from the YÖK Atlas JSON API using the `httr2` package in R. Among the three sources, the API data is the most reliable since it comes straight from the official system, while the third-party 2021 dataset required extra verification during preprocessing.

References: YÖK Atlas (https://yokatlas.yok.gov.tr/), thestats package (https://github.com/analyticsresearchlab/thestats), MorphaxTheDeveloper dataset (https://github.com/MorphaxTheDeveloper/yokatlas-dataset-2025)

title: Trends in University Department Preferences in Turkey (2018–2025)
text:
2.3 Data Overview


Data overview
# Programs per year
df |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(n_programs = n(),
            avg_puan = round(mean(puan, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
            avg_sira = round(mean(sira, na.rm = TRUE), 0)) |>
  print()


# A tibble: 8 × 4
   year n_programs avg_puan avg_sira
  <int>      <int>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1  2018       5935     323.   198128
2  2019       6764     324.   239592
3  2020       9571     318.   312161
4  2021      18357     175    329515
5  2022       8354     284    359386
6  2023       8927     272.   382464
7  2024       9640     243.   391498
8  2025       7597     334.   336911


Data overview
# Programs per category
df |>
  filter(kategori != "Diger") |>
  group_by(kategori) |>
  summarise(n_programs = n(),
            avg_puan = round(mean(puan, na.rm = TRUE), 1)) |>
  arrange(desc(avg_puan)) |>
  print()


# A tibble: 7 × 3
  kategori                n_programs avg_puan
  <chr>                        <int>    <dbl>
1 Tip                           1323     433.
2 Dis_Hekimligi_Eczacilik       1673     396.
3 Hukuk                         1178     350.
4 Ogretmenlik                   4204     323.
5 Psikoloji                     2801     306.
6 Muhendislik                  13495     291.
7 Mimarlik                      3106     266.


The dataset covers eight academic years and contains records from all seven field categories. Medicine has the highest average admission score at 433, followed by dentistry/pharmacy at 396, while architecture sits at the bottom with 266. Engineering, despite having the largest number of programs by far — over 13,000 records across all years — averages around 291, which reflects the wide range of programs it includes. The 2021 row shows an unusually high program count of 18,357, which is likely an artifact of how the third-party data source for that year was structured rather than a real spike in offerings.

title: Trends in University Department Preferences in Turkey (2018–2025)
text:
3.2 Category Trend Analysis


Average score trend by category
# Average minimum score by category over years
category_trend <- df |>
  filter(kategori != "Diger") |>
  group_by(year, kategori) |>
  summarise(avg_puan = mean(puan, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(category_trend, aes(x = year, y = avg_puan, color = kategori)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2018:2025) +
  labs(title = "Average Minimum Admission Score by Category (2018-2025)",
       x = "Year", y = "Average Min Score", color = "Category") +
  theme_minimal()









The most striking feature of this chart is the sharp drop across all categories in 2021, followed by a partial recovery in 2022. This pattern is consistent with a data source change for 2021 rather than a real-year effect, and should be interpreted with caution. Setting that aside, medicine and dentistry/pharmacy have maintained the highest scores throughout the period and both show an upward push toward 2025. Law recovered strongly after 2022 and is trending upward. Engineering and architecture have remained relatively flat, while psychology and teaching stay in the lower range with little clear directional movement.


Number of programs by category
# Program count trend
program_count <- df |>
  filter(kategori != "Diger") |>
  group_by(year, kategori) |>
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(program_count, aes(x = year, y = n, color = kategori)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2018:2025) +
  labs(title = "Number of Programs by Category (2018-2025)",
       x = "Year", y = "Number of Programs", color = "Category") +
  theme_minimal()









Engineering dominates the program count chart throughout the entire period, reaching a peak of around 2,300 programs in 2024 before dropping in 2025. Teaching is the second largest category, though it shows a sharp decline after 2021 — possibly due to quota reductions by the government. Psychology has grown modestly but steadily, going from near the bottom of the chart in 2018 to clearly separating itself from the smaller categories by 2024. Dentistry/pharmacy and medicine remain flat and low in count, which is consistent with the controlled and limited capacity of those fields.

title: Trends in University Department Preferences in Turkey (2018–2025)
Trends in University Department Preferences in Turkey (2018–2025)
Trends in University Department Preferences in Turkey (2018–2025)
text:
3.5 Trend Prediction


Linear trend model for Computer Engineering
# Simple linear model for Bilgisayar Muh.
bilgisayar <- df |>
  filter(bolum_grubu == "Bilgisayar Muh.") |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(avg_puan = mean(puan, na.rm = TRUE))

model <- lm(avg_puan ~ year, data = bilgisayar)
cat("Linear model summary:\n")


Linear model summary:


Linear trend model for Computer Engineering
cat("  Coefficient (yearly change):", round(coef(model)[2], 2), "\n")


  Coefficient (yearly change): -3.38 


Linear trend model for Computer Engineering
cat("  R-squared:", round(summary(model)$r.squared, 3), "\n")


  R-squared: 0.028 


Linear trend model for Computer Engineering
# Predict 2026
pred_2026 <- predict(model, newdata = data.frame(year = 2026))
cat("  Predicted 2026 avg score:", round(pred_2026, 1), "\n")


  Predicted 2026 avg score: 332.6 


Linear trend model for Computer Engineering
ggplot(bilgisayar, aes(x = year, y = avg_puan)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#2E86AB") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "#E74C3C", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2018:2026, limits = c(2018, 2026)) +
  labs(title = "Computer Engineering: Score Trend with Linear Prediction",
       x = "Year", y = "Average Min Score",
       subtitle = paste0("Predicted 2026 score: ", round(pred_2026, 1))) +
  theme_minimal()


`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









The linear model fitted to computer engineering scores actually shows a slightly negative slope, meaning the trend line is gently declining over the 2018–2025 period. This is largely driven by the 2021 outlier and the low 2024 value, which pull the regression line down despite the strong recovery in 2025. The predicted 2026 score of 332.6 is therefore lower than the most recent observed value of around 405, which suggests the model is not capturing the actual recent momentum well. The confidence interval is also very wide, indicating high uncertainty. This analysis serves as a useful exercise in trend modeling, but a more robust approach — such as excluding the anomalous 2021 data point or using a nonlinear model — would give a more reliable forecast.
